response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend hours every day scrolling through social media, and honestly the amount of garbage fake content out there is insane. As someone who works with their hands fixing pipes all day in Rome, I deal with real problems that need real solutions, so when I see people sharing obvious nonsense online it really gets under my skin. You cannot just believe everything you see on your feed. I have learned this the hard way after seeing so many wild stories that turned out to be completely made up. The thing is, most people just share whatever confirms what they already think without checking if it is actually true. I do not have patience for that kind of lazy thinking. When I am working on a plumbing job, I cannot just guess where the leak is or assume the problem based on what I want to believe. I have to actually investigate and find the truth, and people need to apply that same logic to what they see online. My approach is pretty straightforward and I am not going to sugarcoat it. First, if something seems too crazy or too perfect, it probably is fake. Second, check who is posting it. Is it some random account with no credibility or an actual reliable source? Third, do a quick search to see if real news outlets are reporting the same thing. It takes maybe two minutes. I know not everyone has the same education level as others, and I only finished lower secondary school myself, but common sense is free. You do not need a degree to spot obvious lies. What really bothers me is when fake content spreads in communities I care about, whether that is among other immigrants here in Rome or in my volunteering groups. I will call it out directly because staying quiet just lets the problem get worse. Some people get offended when I tell them they shared fake news, but honestly I would rather have an uncomfortable conversation than let misinformation spread. The platforms themselves need to do better too, but we cannot just wait around for them to fix everything. We have to take responsibility for what we share and consume.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 24-year-old non-binary plumber living in Rome who spends several hours daily on social media. Key traits include very high extraversion (score 100), very low agreeableness (score 3), low openness (score 16), and moderate conscientiousness (score 50). The low agreeableness suggests a direct, blunt communication style without much concern for others' feelings. High extraversion indicates confidence in expressing opinions. Low openness suggests preference for practical, straightforward approaches over abstract thinking. The occupation as a plumber provides practical problem-solving metaphors. Lower secondary education level means avoiding overly academic language. Being an immigrant and involved in volunteering provides community context. The widowed status at young age may contribute to a no-nonsense attitude. Time spent on social media makes them familiar with the problem. Low neuroticism means they stay calm and direct rather than anxious about the issue.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, practical, confident, somewhat confrontational, no-nonsense, based on hands-on experience rather than theory
